title: Affirmative action
description: Explain how the Court has at times allowed the restriction of the civil rights of minority groups and at other times has protected those rights.
topic_path: What is affirmative action, and how does it impact society?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Affirmative action is best defined as which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A policy benefiting those who tend to suffer from discrimination or are members of a historically disadvantaged group
A policy creating racial quotas for admitted classes at major colleges and universities for members of disadvantaged groups
A set of guidelines issued by government agencies, which provide specific details about how a policy will be implemented
A law that prohibits segregation of public facilities, as well as discrimination on the basis of race, color, sex, religion, or national origin

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Constitution instructs all who act for the government that they may not ‘deny to any person . . . the equal protection of the laws.’ In implementing this equality instruction, as I see it, government decisionmakers may properly distinguish between policies of exclusion and inclusion. Actions designed to burden groups long denied full citizenship stature are not sensibly ranked with measures taken to hasten the day when entrenched discrimination and its after effects have been extirpated.”

> Associate Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg, dissenting opinion in *Gratz v. Bollinger* (2003)

**Which of the following statements is most consistent with the author’s argument in this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution only forbids racial classifications when they are designed to harm minorities, not help them
The Constitution is colorblind and any racial classifications, even when used to help minorities, are harmful to society
The Constitution allows for the use of race-based quotas as a way to remedy past injustices
The Constitution sufficiently protects people of color and the Court should strike down any policy that aims to give certain people an advantage

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Constitution instructs all who act for the government that they may not ‘deny to any person . . . the equal protection of the laws.’ In implementing this equality instruction, as I see it, government decisionmakers may properly distinguish between policies of exclusion and inclusion. Actions designed to burden groups long denied full citizenship stature are not sensibly ranked with measures taken to hasten the day when entrenched discrimination and its after effects have been extirpated.”

> Associate Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg, dissenting opinion in *Gratz v. Bollinger* (2003)

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colleges and universities should be allowed to use race as a factor in admissions
Colleges and universities should be forbidden from taking race into consideration
Affirmative action policies are an example of reverse racism and violate the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment
While some forms of affirmative action are consistutional, using quota systems based on race is not

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Constitution abhors classifications based on race, not only because those classifications can harm favored races or are based on illegitimate motives, but also because every time the government places citizens on racial registers and makes race relevant to the provision of burdens or benefits, it demeans us all. . . .  Undoubtedly there are other ways to ‘better’ the education of law students aside from ensuring that the student body contains a ‘critical mass’ of underrepresented minority students."

> Associate Justice Clarence Thomas, dissenting opinion in *Grutter v. Bollinger* (2003)

**Which of the following statements is most consistent with the author’s argument in this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution is colorblind and any racial classifications, even when used to help minorities, are harmful to society
The Constitution only forbids racial classifications when they are designed to harm minorities, not help them
The Constitution does not include enough protections for people of color and the Supreme Court has to rectify that
The Constitution allows for the use of race-based quotas as a way to remedy past injustices

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Constitution abhors classifications based on race, not only because those classifications can harm favored races or are based on illegitimate motives, but also because every time the government places citizens on racial registers and makes race relevant to the provision of burdens or benefits, it demeans us all. . . .  Undoubtedly there are other ways to ‘better’ the education of law students aside from ensuring that the student body contains a ‘critical mass’ of underrepresented minority students.

> Associate Justice Clarence Thomas, dissenting opinion in *Grutter v. Bollinger* (2003)

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colleges and universities should be forbidden from taking race into consideration
Colleges and universities should be allowed to use race as a factor in admissions
Affirmative action policies are necessary to address past racial injustices and level the playing field
Affirmative action policies are necessary now to help people of color, but will not be necessary in twenty-five years

assessment_items.question_content:
>“As this Court’s cases have made clear, however, the compelling interest that justifies consideration of race in college admissions is not an interest in enrolling a certain number of minority students. Rather, a university may institute a race-conscious admissions program as a means of obtaining ‘the educational benefits that flow from student body diversity’. . . .
> “Increasing minority enrollment may be instrumental to these educational benefits, but it is not, as petitioner seems to suggest, a goal that can or should be reduced to pure numbers. ”

> Associate Justice Anthony Kennedy, opinion of the court in *Fisher v. University of Texas* (2013)

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colleges and universities should be allowed to use race as a factor in admissions
Colleges and universities should be forbidden from taking race into consideration for student admissions
Affirmative action policies give an unfair advantage to people of color for admission to colleges and universities
Affirmative action policies are an example of reverse racism and violate the equal protection clause of the 14th Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
>“As this Court’s cases have made clear, however, the compelling interest that justifies consideration of race in college admissions is not an interest in enrolling a certain number of minority students. Rather, a university may institute a race-conscious admissions program as a means of obtaining ‘the educational benefits that flow from student body diversity’. . . .
> “Increasing minority enrollment may be instrumental to these educational benefits, but it is not, as petitioner seems to suggest, a goal that can or should be reduced to pure numbers.”

> Associate Justice Anthony Kennedy, opinion of the court in *Fisher v. University of Texas* (2013)

**Which of the following statements is most consistent with the author’s argument in this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution only forbids racial classifications when they are designed to harm minorities, not help them
The Constitution is colorblind and any racial classifications, even when used to help minorities, are harmful to society
The Constitution does not include enough protections for people of color and the Court has to rectify that
The Constitution allows for the use of race-based quotas as a way to remedy past injustices

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would be considered a violation of the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A university establishes race-based quotas in their admission policy
A college uses race as a factor in their admission policy
The police inspects the contents of a backpack without a warrant
The state refuses to provide a lawyer to someone who cannot afford one

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

![Table of results showing answers to two questions: The first question, asked on Sept. 5 2007, was: "To overcome past discrimination, do you favor Affirmative Action Programs to help blacks get better jobs/education?" Responses: 60% of total respondents said they favored such measures, with 52% of whites, 89% of blacks, and 77% of Hispanics responding that they favored such measures. 30% of total respondents said they opposed such measures, with 37% of whites, 6% of blacks, and 17% of Hispanics responding that they opposed such measures. 10% of total respondents said they did not know if they favored or opposed such measures, with 11% of whites, 5% of blacks, and 6% of Hispanics saying they did not know. There were 1541 total respondents to this question, 767 of which were white, 498 of which were black, and 198 of which were Hispanic. The second question, asked on Oct. 6, 2007, was: "To overcome past discrimination, do you favor affirmative action programs which give special preferences to qualified blacks in hiring/education? Responses: 46% of total respondents said they favored such measures, with 39% of whites, 78% of blacks, and 61% of Hispanics reporting they favored such measures. 40% of total respondents said they opposed such measures, with 47% of whites, 13% of blacks, and 26% of Hispanics saying they opposed such measures. 14% of total respondents said they did not know if they favored or opposed such measures, with 14% of whites, 9% of blacks, and 13% of Hispanics saying they did not know if they supported such measures. There were 1545 respondents to this question, 769 of which were white, 509 of which were black, and 190 of which were Hispanic.  ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/888b5a2fd7a1b13950f321e2b90afa08.gif)
Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.pewresearch.org/2009/06/02/public-backs-affirmative-action-but-not-minority-preferences/)  

In the public opinion poll described in the table above, two groups of approximately 1500 people were asked if they favored or opposed the following statements: 

Statement A: To overcome past discrimination, do you favor affirmative action programs to help blacks get better jobs/education? 

Result: 60% of respondents said they favored these programs. 

Statement B: To overcome past discrimination, do you favor affirmative action programs which give special preferences to qualified blacks in hiring/education? 

Result: 46% of respondents said they favored these programs. 

**Which of the following most likely accounts for the different results?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The questions contained a difference in wording
There were no black respondents in one of the samples
The two groups surveyed had significantly different racial demographic characteristics
The two surveys were conducted several years apart

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

![Table of results showing answers to two questions: The first question, asked on Sept. 5 2007, was: "To overcome past discrimination, do you favor Affirmative Action Programs to help blacks get better jobs/education?" Responses: 60% of total respondents said they favored such measures, with 52% of whites, 89% of blacks, and 77% of Hispanics responding that they favored such measures. 30% of total respondents said they opposed such measures, with 37% of whites, 6% of blacks, and 17% of Hispanics responding that they opposed such measures. 10% of total respondents said they did not know if they favored or opposed such measures, with 11% of whites, 5% of blacks, and 6% of Hispanics saying they did not know. There were 1541 total respondents to this question, 767 of which were white, 498 of which were black, and 198 of which were Hispanic. The second question, asked on Oct. 6, 2007, was: "To overcome past discrimination, do you favor affirmative action programs which give special preferences to qualified blacks in hiring/education? Responses: 46% of total respondents said they favored such measures, with 39% of whites, 78% of blacks, and 61% of Hispanics reporting they favored such measures. 40% of total respondents said they opposed such measures, with 47% of whites, 13% of blacks, and 26% of Hispanics saying they opposed such measures. 14% of total respondents said they did not know if they favored or opposed such measures, with 14% of whites, 9% of blacks, and 13% of Hispanics saying they did not know if they supported such measures. There were 1545 respondents to this question, 769 of which were white, 509 of which were black, and 190 of which were Hispanic.  ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/888b5a2fd7a1b13950f321e2b90afa08.gif)
Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.pewresearch.org/2009/06/02/public-backs-affirmative-action-but-not-minority-preferences/)  

**The data displayed in the table above best supports which of the following statements?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Respondents of all races were less likely to support programs giving special preferences to black candidates than programs aimed to help them more generally
Hispanic respondents were less likely to favor programs helping black candidates than programs helping Hispanic candidates
White respondents were more likely to favor programs giving special preferences to black candidates than programs aimed to help them more generally
Black respondents were more likely to oppose programs giving special preferences to black candidates than Hispanic respondents

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Race-based action necessary to further a compelling governmental interest does not violate the Equal Protection Clause so long as it is narrowly tailored to further that interest. Context matters when reviewing such action. Not every decision influenced by race is equally objectionable, and strict scrutiny is designed to provide a framework for carefully examining the importance and the sincerity of the government's reasons for using race in a particular context.”

>-Justice Sandra Day O’Connor, writing for the Court in *Grutter v. Bollinger* (2003) 

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution does not always forbid decisions based on race
The Equal Protection Clause prohibits racial classifications in all cases
There is no context in which the government has a compelling interest in making racial classifications
The Constitution permits wide-ranging classifications by race

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Universities cannot establish quotas for members of certain racial or ethnic groups or put them on separate admissions tracks. The Law School's admissions program . . . satisfies these requirements. Moreover, the program is flexible enough to ensure that each applicant is evaluated as an individual and not in a way that makes race or ethnicity the defining feature of the application. The Law School engages in a highly individualized, holistic review of each applicant's file, giving serious consideration to all the ways an applicant might contribute to a diverse educational environment.”

>Justice Sandra Day O’Connor, writing for the Court in *Grutter v. Bollinger* (2003) 

**Which of the following governmental policies would the author most likely support?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A state university admissions program that takes race into account as part of an individual’s overall record
A federal hiring program that reserves 20% of positions for minority applicants
A national scholarship program that is completely colorblind in giving out awards
A state medical school admissions program that has different requirements for male and female applicants
